title: The trigger of being asked what you want or need
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The question that stops you cold 
 Someone asks you what you want for dinner. Or what you need this week. Or what would feel good right now. And your mind...
htmlContent:
The question that stops you cold

Someone asks you what you want for dinner. Or what you need this week. Or what would feel good right now. And your mind goes completely blank. Not the kind of blank where you're thinking and deciding. The kind where there's nothing there. A white wall. Static. A strange hollow feeling in your chest.

I know this experience because I've lived it. For years, that question felt like a trap. Like being handed a test in a language I'd never studied. Here's the thing: this response is not a personality flaw. It's not that you're indecisive or passive or weak. It's a survival adaptation. And understanding that changes everything.

When you grow up in an environment where your needs were ignored, punished, or used against you, your nervous system learns a dangerous lesson. Wanting things is unsafe. Having needs is dangerous. Expressing what you actually feel is a liability. So your brain does something incredibly smart. It shuts down the entire system that generates wants and needs. Poof. Gone. Like a circuit breaker that trips before the house burns down.

This is what trauma researchers call the freeze response. Stephen Porges, the man behind polyvagal theory, would explain it as your ventral vagal pathway being blocked. Your social engagement system gets overridden by older, more primitive survival circuits. You literally cannot access the part of your brain that knows what you want because that part got wired to danger.

Sit with that for a second. Your nervous system protected you by erasing your desires. That's not weakness. That's a miracle of adaptation.

The biology of not knowing

Let's get specific about what happens in your body when someone asks you what you need. Your heart might speed up slightly. Your shoulders tighten. Maybe your throat closes a little. There's a familiar pressure behind your eyes or a hollow feeling in your stomach. These are not random sensations. They are your nervous system saying "danger, danger, this question is unsafe."

I remember sitting in a coffee shop with a friend who asked me what kind of drink I wanted. Simple question. Normal question. And I felt like I was being interrogated. My body went rigid. I said "whatever you're having" with a smile that hurt my face. That moment cost me something I didn't even know I was paying.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. Not in your memories or your thoughts. In your tissues, your fascia, your nervous system. The question "what do you want?" isn't just a question. It's a trigger that activates your body's memory of every time wanting something led to pain. Every time you expressed a need and got ignored. Every time you asked for something and got punished. Every time your desires were treated as an inconvenience or a burden.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It's not a fix, but it can take the edge off that fight-or-flight activation when you're working with these questions. Your nervous system needs to feel safe enough to even begin the process of discovering what you want.

The really wild part is that this shutdown response becomes automatic. It happens in milliseconds. Faster than conscious thought. By the time you realize you don't know what you want, the decision has already been made by your survival brain. You don't want anything because wanting anything was too dangerous. Your brain chose safety over desire. Every single time.

How complex trauma rewires your desire circuits

If you grew up with emotional neglect, your wants and needs were never mirrored back to you. A healthy caregiver notices when you're hungry and says "you look hungry, let's get you some food." They notice when you're tired and say "you seem tired, time for rest." They notice when you're sad and say "you look sad, tell me about it." Through this mirroring, you learn what you feel and what you need. You build an internal map of your own experience.

Without that mirroring, you never developed that map. You learned to look outward for cues about what you should want. You learned to scan other people's faces for what was acceptable. You learned to ask "what do you want?" because your own wants were a foreign country you'd never been given a passport to visit.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn's work on inherited family trauma explains how these patterns get passed down through generations. The inability to know what you want might not even be yours alone. It might be a gift from your grandmother who learned that wanting things got her hurt. It might be a legacy from your father who was never allowed to have needs of his own.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his research at the Trauma Center in Boston, found that trauma literally changes the structure of your brain. The medial prefrontal cortex, which helps you know what you feel and what matters to you, gets suppressed. The insula, which maps your internal body states, gets disconnected from conscious awareness. You quite literally lose access to the information your body is giving you about what you need.

Think about that. Your brain changed shape to protect you. The architecture of your desire was dismantled for your survival. That's not a character flaw. That's a structural adaptation to an impossible situation.

Starting the slow work of coming back

So where do you start when you don't know what you want? How do you rebuild something that was dismantled before you had words for it? The answer is not pretty or fast. It's slow. It's boring. It's frustrating. And it's the only thing that works.

Start with your body, not your mind. Your mind will give you nothing because your mind learned that wanting is dangerous. But your body still knows. Your body knows when it's hungry. When it's tired. When it needs to move. When it needs to rest. These are the smallest, most basic wants. And they are the foundation everything else builds on.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). Lengthening your exhale activates the vagus nerve and signals safety to your nervous system. You need that safety signal before your brain will even consider letting desire back online. You cannot force yourself to know what you want. You can only create conditions where wanting becomes possible again.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you feel resistance when I said "start with your body"? Did you feel that familiar urge to intellectualize or dismiss? That's okay. That's the protective part of you that learned that body awareness was dangerous. Thank it for keeping you safe. And then try anyway.

Here's a practice that helped me. Set a timer for two minutes. Sit quietly. Don't try to know what you want. Just notice what your body is experiencing. A pressure in your chest. A tightness in your jaw. A heaviness in your legs. That's it. That's all you have to do. You're not trying to find answers. You're just rebuilding the connection between your conscious mind and your internal body states. You're rewiring the insula. You're telling your brain "it's safe to feel again."

The grief you will meet along the way

I need to be honest with you about something. When you start this work, you will meet grief. Deep, old grief. Because once you start to reconnect with what you want, you will also feel the pain of all the years you couldn't. All the desires that were buried alive. All the needs that were never met. All the moments you gave yourself away because you didn't know you had a self to give.

This grief is not a sign that you're doing something wrong. It's a sign that you're doing something right. It's the price of admission to your own life.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). Jonice Webb's work on childhood emotional neglect names this experience directly. The feeling of being empty. The sense that something is missing but you can't name it. The terror of being asked what you want because there's nothing there to find. She calls it "the invisible cage." I've never heard a better description.

Stay with me here. The goal is not to suddenly know what you want for every meal and every career decision and every relationship. The goal is to slowly, painfully, beautifully rebuild the capacity to even ask the question without your nervous system screaming "danger." The goal is to sit with the question long enough that your body learns it's not a trap.

You might start with something as small as choosing between two colors of socks. Or deciding what temperature you want the water in your shower. Or noticing that you actually do want the window open, not closed. These tiny decisions are not trivial. They are neural pathways being rebuilt. They are your nervous system learning a new language. The language of your own desire.

The open ending you deserve

I don't have a neat conclusion for you. This work doesn't end. You don't arrive at a place where you always know what you want and never feel that blankness again. What happens is that the blankness gets a little less terrifying. The pause gets a little shorter. The voice that says "I don't know" gets a little quieter, and the voice that says "maybe this" gets a little louder.

Some days you will know exactly what you want for dinner and it will feel like a miracle. Other days you will be asked what movie you want to watch and you'll feel that familiar white wall. Both days are okay. Both days are part of the process.

The question "what do you want or need" might always carry some charge for you. But that charge can change. It can move from terror to curiosity. From shutdown to possibility. From "I don't know" to "I'm learning."

You are learning the language of your own body. You are rebuilding a relationship with your own desire. You are coming home to a self that was never really gone, just hidden. Hidden for good reason. Hidden for survival. But ready, now, to be found.
